title: The Kingdom of Namayan
description: Taytay flourishes as a pre-Hispanic barangay composed of 30 to 100 families. It is a vital part of the Kingdom of Namayan, engaging in active barter trade with Chinese and Arab merchants along the waterways.

title: Franciscan Mission
description: Franciscan missionaries arrive and officially establish the town of Taytay, founding the Parish of St. John the Baptist and making it one of the oldest settlements in the region.

title: The Encomienda System
description: Spanish records show the Encomienda of Taitay (Taytay) belonging to the Province of Tondo. By 1591, it is placed under the jurisdiction of the Franciscan Order in the Province of La Laguna.

title: Transfer to the Jesuits
description: The administration of Taytay is transferred to the Jesuits. Due to frequent flooding near the shores of Laguna de Bay, the town center and the church are eventually relocated to their present higher location in 1632.

title: Distrito Politico-Militar de Morong
description: A new political subdivision is formed. Taytay, along with Antipolo, Bosoboso, and Cainta, is separated from the Province of Tondo to become part of this new district, with Morong as its capital.

title: Philippine-American War
description: Taytay becomes a site of conflict during the Philippine-American War. Local forces fight bravely before the town is eventually captured by American troops.

title: Creation of Rizal Province
description: On June 11, following a historic meeting and acrimonious debate at the Pasig Catholic Church, the First Philippine Commission enacts Act No. 137. The Province of Rizal is officially created, and Taytay is incorporated as one of its 26 municipalities.

title: Remaining in Rizal
description: Under Presidential Decree No. 824, 12 towns are carved out to form the new Metro Manila Area. Taytay is one of the 14 remaining towns that continue to compose the Province of Rizal.
